content:
# Genesis Chapter 10 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 6r-6v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_010_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_010_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 7-8 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**10:1** And these are the generations [וְאֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת] of the sons of Noah [בְּנֵי-נֹחַ] — Shem [שֵׁם], Ham [חָם], and Japheth [וָיָפֶת]; and sons were born to them [וַיִּוָּלְדוּ לָהֶם בָּנִים] after the flood [אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל].

---

*The Sons of Japheth (vv. 2–5)*

**10:2** The sons of Japheth [בְּנֵי יֶפֶת]: Gomer [גֹּמֶר] and Magog [וּמָגוֹג] and Madai [וּמָדַי] and Javan [וְיָוָן] and Tubal [וְתֻבָל] and Meshech [וָמֶשֶׁךְ] and Tiras [וָתִירָס].

**10:3** And the sons of Gomer [וּבְנֵי גֹמֶר]: Ashkenaz [אַשְׁכְּנַז] and Riphath [וְרִיפַת] and Togarmah [וְתֹגַרְמָה].

**10:4** And the sons of Javan [וּבְנֵי יָוָן]: Elishah [אֱלִישָׁה] and Tarshish [וְתַרְשִׁישׁ], Kittim [כִּתִּים] and Dodanim [וְדֹדָנִים].

**10:5** From these [מֵאֵלֶּה] the coastland peoples [נִפְרְדוּ אִיֵּי הַגּוֹיִם] were spread [נִפְרְדוּ] in their lands [בְּאַרְצֹתָם], each according to his tongue [אִישׁ לִלְשֹׁנוֹ], according to their families [לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם] in their nations [בְּגוֹיֵהֶם].

---

*The Sons of Ham (vv. 6–20)*

**10:6** And the sons of Ham [וּבְנֵי חָם]: Cush [כּוּשׁ] and Mizraim [וּמִצְרַיִם] and Put [וּפוּט] and Canaan [וּכְנָעַן].

**10:7** And the sons of Cush [וּבְנֵי כוּשׁ]: Seba [סְבָא] and Havilah [וַחֲוִילָה] and Sabtah [וְסַבְתָּה] and Raamah [וְרַעְמָה] and Sabteca [וְסַבְתְּכָא]; and the sons of Raamah [וּבְנֵי רַעְמָה]: Sheba [שְׁבָא] and Dedan [וּדְדָן].

**10:8** And Cush fathered [וְכוּשׁ יָלַד] Nimrod [אֶת-נִמְרֹד]; he was the first [הוּא הֵחֵל] to be a mighty one [לִהְיוֹת גִּבֹּר] in the earth [בָּאָרֶץ].

**10:9** He was a mighty hunter [גִּבֹּר-צַיִד] before the LORD [לִפְנֵי יְהוָה]; therefore it is said [עַל-כֵּן יֵאָמַר]: Like Nimrod [כְּנִמְרֹד], a mighty hunter before the LORD [גִּבֹּר צַיִד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה].

**10:10** And the beginning of his kingdom [וַתְּהִי רֵאשִׁית מַמְלַכְתּוֹ] was Babel [בָּבֶל] and Erech [וְאֶרֶךְ] and Accad [וְאַכַּד] and Calneh [וְכַלְנֵה] in the land of Shinar [בְּאֶרֶץ שִׁנְעָר].

**10:11** From that land [מִן-הָאָרֶץ הַהִוא] Asshur went out [יָצָא אַשּׁוּר] and built Nineveh [וַיִּבֶן אֶת-נִינְוֵה] and the city Rehoboth [וְאֶת-רְחֹבֹת עִיר] and Calah [וְאֶת-כָּלַח],

**10:12** and Resen [וְאֶת-רֶסֶן] between Nineveh and between Calah [בֵּין נִינְוֵה וּבֵין כָּלַח] — that is the great city [הִוא הָעִיר הַגְּדֹלָה].

**10:13** And Mizraim fathered [וּמִצְרַיִם יָלַד] Ludim [אֶת-לוּדִים] and Anamim [וְאֶת-עֲנָמִים] and Lehabim [וְאֶת-לְהָבִים] and Naphtuhim [וְאֶת-נַפְתֻּחִים],

**10:14** and Pathrusim [וְאֶת-פַּתְרֻסִים] and Casluhim [וְאֶת-כַּסְלֻחִים] — from whom went out the Philistines [אֲשֶׁר יָצְאוּ מִשָּׁם פְּלִשְׁתִּים] — and Caphtorim [וְאֶת-כַּפְתֹּרִים].

**10:15** And Canaan fathered [וּכְנַעַן יָלַד] Sidon [אֶת-צִידֹן] his firstborn [בְּכֹרוֹ] and Heth [וְאֶת-חֵת],

**10:16** and the Jebusite [וְאֶת-הַיְבוּסִי] and the Amorite [וְאֶת-הָאֱמֹרִי] and the Girgashite [וְאֶת-הַגִּרְגָּשִׁי],

**10:17** and the Hivite [וְאֶת-הַחִוִּי] and the Arkite [וְאֶת-הָעַרְקִי] and the Sinite [וְאֶת-הַסִּינִי],

**10:18** and the Arvadite [וְאֶת-הָאַרְוָדִי] and the Zemarite [וְאֶת-הַצְּמָרִי] and the Hamathite [וְאֶת-הַחֲמָתִי]; and afterward [וְאַחַר] the families of the Canaanites [נָפֹצוּ מִשְׁפְּחוֹת הַכְּנַעֲנִי] were scattered abroad [נָפֹצוּ].

**10:19** And the border of the Canaanites [וַיְהִי גְּבוּל הַכְּנַעֲנִי] was from Sidon [מִצִּידֹן], as you go [בֹּאֲכָה] toward Gerar [גְּרָרָה], as far as Gaza [עַד-עַזָּה]; as you go toward Sodom [בֹּאֲכָה סְדֹמָה] and Gomorrah [וַעֲמֹרָה] and Admah [וְאַדְמָה] and Zeboiim [וּצְבֹיִם], as far as Lasha [עַד-לָשַׁע].

**10:20** These are the sons of Ham [אֵלֶּה בְנֵי-חָם] according to their families [לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם], according to their tongues [לִלְשֹׁנֹתָם] in their lands [בְּאַרְצֹתָם] in their nations [בְּגוֹיֵהֶם].

---

*The Sons of Shem (vv. 21–31)*

**10:21** And to Shem also [וּלְשֵׁם יֻלַּד] — he was the father of all the children of Eber [הוּא אֲבִי כָּל-בְּנֵי-עֵבֶר], the elder brother of Japheth [אֲחִי יֶפֶת הַגָּדוֹל] — even to him [גַּם-לוֹ] children were born [יֻלַּד].

**10:22** The sons of Shem [בְּנֵי שֵׁם]: Elam [עֵילָם] and Asshur [וְאַשּׁוּר] and Arphaxad [וְאַרְפַּכְשַׁד] and Lud [וְלוּד] and Aram [וַאֲרָם].

**10:23** And the sons of Aram [וּבְנֵי אֲרָם]: Uz [עוּץ] and Hul [וְחוּל] and Gether [וְגֶתֶר] and Mash [וָמַשׁ].

**10:24** And Arphaxad fathered [וְאַרְפַּכְשַׁד יָלַד] Shelah [אֶת-שָׁלַח], and Shelah fathered [וְשֶׁלַח יָלַד] Eber [אֶת-עֵבֶר].

**10:25** And to Eber were born [וּלְעֵבֶר יֻלַּד] two sons [שְׁנֵי בָנִים]: the name of the one [שֵׁם הָאֶחָד] was Peleg [פֶּלֶג] — for in his days [כִּי בְיָמָיו] the earth was divided [נִפְלְגָה הָאָרֶץ] — and the name of his brother [וְשֵׁם אָחִיו] was Joktan [יָקְטָן].

**10:26** And Joktan fathered [וְיָקְטָן יָלַד] Almodad [אֶת-אַלְמוֹדָד] and Sheleph [וְאֶת-שָׁלֶף] and Hazarmaveth [וְאֶת-חֲצַרְמָוֶת] and Jerah [וְאֶת-יָרַח],

**10:27** and Hadoram [וְאֶת-הֲדוֹרָם] and Uzal [וְאֶת-אוּזָל] and Diklah [וְאֶת-דִּקְלָה],

**10:28** and Obal [וְאֶת-עוֹבָל] and Abimael [וְאֶת-אֲבִימָאֵל] and Sheba [וְאֶת-שְׁבָא],

**10:29** and Ophir [וְאֶת-אוֹפִר] and Havilah [וְאֶת-חֲוִילָה] and Jobab [וְאֶת-יוֹבָב]; all these were the sons of Joktan [כָּל-אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי יָקְטָן].

**10:30** And their dwelling [וַיְהִי מוֹשָׁבָם] was from Mesha [מִמֵּשָׁא], as you go [בֹּאֲכָה] toward Sephar [סְפָרָה], the mountain of the east [הַר הַקֶּדֶם].

**10:31** These are the sons of Shem [אֵלֶּה בְנֵי-שֵׁם] according to their families [לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם] according to their tongues [לִלְשֹׁנֹתָם] in their lands [בְּאַרְצֹתָם] according to their nations [לְגוֹיֵהֶם].

---

*Conclusion (v. 32)*

**10:32** These are the families of the sons of Noah [אֵלֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹת בְּנֵי-נֹחַ] according to their generations [לְתוֹלְדֹתָם] in their nations [בְּגוֹיֵהֶם]; and from these [וּמֵאֵלֶּה] the nations were spread abroad [נִפְרְדוּ הַגּוֹיִם] in the earth after the flood [בָּאָרֶץ אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 10:1 — וְאֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת: The Fourth Toledot Formula

וְאֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת בְּנֵי-נֹחַ שֵׁם חָם וָיָפֶת — "And these are the generations of the sons of Noah — Shem, Ham, and Japheth."

This is the fourth occurrence of the toledot [תּוֹלְדֹת] formula in Genesis (previously: 2:4 heavens/earth; 5:1 Adam; 6:9 Noah). The toledot formula functions as the primary structural device of the book. Here it opens the great ethnographic chapter — the Table of Nations — which is one of the most remarkable documents in ancient literature: an attempt to account for the origin of all known peoples from a single ancestor.

The order of sons — Shem, Ham, Japheth — is the canonical order used throughout the genealogies of Genesis. Yet the chapter proceeds in the reverse order: Japheth first (vv. 2-5), then Ham (vv. 6-20), then Shem last (vv. 21-31). This reversed treatment saves Shem for last — the line of the covenant, the ancestor of Abraham — as the climax of the chapter.

**"And sons were born to them after the flood" (וַיִּוָּלְדוּ לָהֶם בָּנִים אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל):** The sons listed in what follows are not the sons born before the flood (who boarded the ark as adults) but the descendants born in the post-flood era. The Table of Nations is thus a post-flood document of the second generation and beyond.

**LXX 10:1:** αὗται δὲ αἱ γενέσεις τῶν υἱῶν Νωε — "And these are the generations of the sons of Noah." The LXX uses γενέσεις (geneses, origins/generations) consistently for תּוֹלְדֹת throughout Genesis. The LXX adds: Σημ Χαμ Ιαφεθ, καὶ ἐγεννήθησαν αὐτοῖς υἱοὶ μετὰ τὸν κατακλυσμόν — conforming exactly to the MT.

---

### On 10:2-5 — The Japhethite Nations: Peoples of the Coastlands

The sons of Japheth represent, in the ancient Israelite geographic horizon, the peoples of the north and northwest — Anatolia, the Aegean, the Iranian plateau, and beyond. The identifications below follow the most widely accepted scholarly correspondences, though many remain disputed:

| Hebrew | Greek | Identification |
|--------|-------|---------------|
| גֹּמֶר (Gomer) | Γαμερ | The Cimmerians (Akkadian: Gimirrai); nomadic people north of the Black Sea, later in Anatolia |
| מָגוֹג (Magog) | Μαγωγ | Uncertain; possibly the Scythians (Akkadian: Magugu); associated in Ezek. 38-39 with a northern eschatological power |
| מָדַי (Madai) | Μαδαι | The Medes (Old Persian: Mada); Iranian plateau people, later part of the Medo-Persian empire |
| יָוָן (Javan) | Ιαουαν | Ionia / the Greeks (Akkadian: Iamnaya; Sanskrit: Yavana); the Aegean Greeks |
| תֻּבָל (Tubal) | Θοβελ | The Tabali of Assyrian records; people of eastern Anatolia (possibly Cappadocia or the Black Sea region) |
| מֶשֶׁךְ (Meshech) | Μοσοχ | The Mushki of Assyrian records; people of central/eastern Anatolia; associated with Tubal in Ezek. 27:13; 38:2 |
| תִּירָס (Tiras) | Θειρας | Uncertain; possibly the Tyrsenoi (Etruscans?) or Thracians |

**Sons of Gomer (v. 3):**
- אַשְׁכְּנַז (Ashkenaz): The Scythians (Akkadian: Ashkuza); later used for the Rhine-Danube region in medieval Jewish usage, hence "Ashkenazic" Jews
- רִיפַת (Riphath): Uncertain; possibly the Paphlagonians or a Black Sea people (1 Chr. 1:6 has "Diphath" — a variant)
- תֹּגַרְמָה (Togarmah): Armenian tradition consistently identifies this as Armenia; Ezek. 27:14 mentions Togarmah as a horse-trading people

**Sons of Javan (v. 4):**
- אֱלִישָׁה (Elishah): Alashiya (Cyprus) in Ugaritic and Egyptian texts; the Aeolian Greeks in some traditions
- תַּרְשִׁישׁ (Tarshish): A distant western Mediterranean locale — Tartessus in southern Spain or Sardinia; the "ships of Tarshish" are the great ocean-going vessels of antiquity
- כִּתִּים (Kittim): Kition in Cyprus; later broadened to mean the Romans (in Daniel and at Qumran), then any great western naval power
- דֹּדָנִים (Dodanim): Probably the Dardanians (Trojans/Danaans area); 1 Chr. 1:7 reads רֹדָנִים (Rodanim — Rhodes), and the LXX supports this reading

**Note on v. 4 — דֹּדָנִים / רֹדָנִים:** The Leningrad Codex (MT) reads דֹּדָנִים; 1 Chronicles 1:7 and the LXX read Ῥόδιοι / Rodanim — a reference to the island of Rhodes. The Qere/Ketiv apparatus may preserve awareness of the variant. The LXX's reading points to Rhodes as the intended referent. EMET follows the MT's כְּתִיב (written form) דֹּדָנִים while noting the variant.

**10:5 — "The coastland peoples were spread" (נִפְרְדוּ אִיֵּי הַגּוֹיִם):**

The phrase אִיֵּי הַגּוֹיִם (the coastlands/islands of the nations) is the Israelite designation for the peoples accessible by sea — the Mediterranean islands, the Aegean coast, the Anatolian seaboard. The verb נָפַר (nafar, "to spread, be separated") is used at the conclusion of both the Japhethite (v. 5) and overall (v. 32) sections, tying the chapter together.

The threefold formula at the close of each section — "according to his tongue / according to their families / in their nations" — is a systematic ethnographic classification by: (1) language, (2) kinship group, (3) political entity. This is a remarkably sophisticated ancient taxonomy that anticipates modern ethnolinguistic classification.

---

### On 10:6 — The Sons of Ham: Africa, Canaan, and the Near East

Ham's descendants occupy a wide geographic range: northeast Africa (Cush/Ethiopia, Mizraim/Egypt, Put/Libya), Canaan, and the Arabian peninsula. The identifications:

| Hebrew | Identification |
|--------|---------------|
| כּוּשׁ (Cush) | Nubia/Ethiopia (south of Egypt); in Assyrian: Kusu |
| מִצְרַיִם (Mizraim) | Egypt (dual form: "the two Egypts" — Upper and Lower Egypt) |
| פּוּט (Put) | Libya (western North Africa); the Libyan people of Egyptian records |
| כְּנַעַן (Canaan) | The Canaanite peoples of the Levant — the land of promise |

The genealogical placement of Canaan under Ham (rather than Shem) is the foundational ethnographic move that makes the Curse of Canaan in 9:25 intelligible within the Table of Nations. Canaan is Ham's son, and Ham's line is cursed through Canaan.

---

### On 10:7 — The Sons of Cush: Arabian and African Peoples

The sons of Cush (v. 7) span both the African and Arabian sides of the Red Sea, reflecting the ancient geographic-cultural corridor between northeast Africa and southwest Arabia:

- סְבָא (Seba): Meroe/Nubian kingdom, or alternatively the Sabeans of south Arabia
- חֲוִילָה (Havilah): A gold-rich land (cf. Gen. 2:11); southwest Arabia (Yemen) or east Africa
- סַבְתָּה (Sabtah): Possibly Shabwat (Shabwa) in south Arabia (Hadramaut)
- רַעְמָה (Raamah): Southwest Arabia; Ragma in the Ptolemaic geography near the Persian Gulf
- סַבְתְּכָא (Sabteca): Unknown; possibly in east Africa or the Persian Gulf region

**Sons of Raamah:** שְׁבָא (Sheba) — the famous Sabean kingdom of southwest Arabia (modern Yemen), known for spices, gold, and the Queen of Sheba (1 Kgs. 10); וּדְדָן (Dedan) — a major north Arabian trading center (modern al-'Ula).

Note that Sheba and Dedan appear both as sons of Raamah (son of Cush, here) and as sons of Jokshan (son of Abraham by Keturah, Gen. 25:3). This double ancestry reflects the complexity of ancient tribal relationships and the overlapping of trade-network identities with genealogical assignment.

---

### On 10:8-12 — The Nimrod Section: Warrior-King and City-Builder

This passage is the most dramatically charged narrative in Genesis 10 and contains some of the most debated Hebrew in the chapter.

**10:8 — הוּא הֵחֵל לִהְיוֹת גִּבֹּר בָּאָרֶץ: "He was the first to be a mighty one in the earth"**

The verb הֵחֵל (hechal, Hiphil of חָלַל, "to begin") introduces Nimrod as a initiator — not merely a warrior but the first [gibbor] warrior. The word גִּבֹּר (gibbor, "mighty one, warrior, hero") is the same word used of the נְפִילִים (Nephilim) in Genesis 6:4: "those were the mighty ones [הַגִּבֹּרִים] who were from of old, men of renown." Nimrod is thus characterized in the same register as the pre-flood giants — a post-flood echo of that antediluvian warrior-hero culture.

**10:9 — גִּבֹּר-צַיִד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה: "A Mighty Hunter Before the LORD"**

The phrase לִפְנֵי יְהוָה ("before the LORD / in the face of YHWH") is interpretively pivotal. Two readings:

1. **Superlative/intensifier:** "Before the LORD" functions as a superlative — the greatest possible hunter, "hunter par excellence." Compare "a great city before God" (Jonah 3:3, עִיר גְּדוֹלָה לֵאלֹהִים). On this reading, the phrase is complimentary: Nimrod was the ultimate hunter — God himself would acknowledge it.

2. **Adversative/defiant:** "Before the LORD" denotes standing in YHWH's face — in his presence, against him, in confrontation with him. Some ancient interpreters (Philo, certain rabbinic traditions) read Nimrod as hunting men rather than animals — as a tyrant who hunted people before (i.e., in defiance of) YHWH. The Targum Pseudo-Jonathan explicitly renders this as "a sinful hunter of men before the LORD."

The EMET translation renders "before the LORD" literally, preserving the ambiguity. The proverbial citation in the second half of v. 9 ("therefore it is said: like Nimrod, a mighty hunter before the LORD") confirms that whatever the phrase means, Nimrod was so famous for it that his name became a byword. The proverb's existence suggests ancient fame, not condemnation — but the context of the Babel narrative immediately following (Genesis 11) colors the Nimrod tradition with imperial overreach.

**The name נִמְרֹד (Nimrod):** The etymology is disputed:
- A popular folk etymology connects it to מָרַד (marad, "to rebel") — "Nimrod the Rebel." This is the reading underlying much ancient and modern anti-Nimrod interpretation.
- Scholarly identification often associates Nimrod with Tukulti-Ninurta I (Assyrian warrior-king, c. 1243-1207 BCE), Sargon of Akkad, or Gilgamesh (the great Mesopotamian hunting-warrior-king).
- The name may be Akkadian in origin: Ninurta was the Mesopotamian god of hunting and warfare, and "Nimrud" (Arabic form) is preserved in the site name of ancient Kalhu (Calah) in Assyria.

**10:10 — The Kingdom of Nimrod: Southern Mesopotamia**

רֵאשִׁית מַמְלַכְתּוֹ (reshit mamlaxtoh, "the beginning of his kingdom") — the four cities:
- בָּבֶל (Babel): Babylon — the great city of lower Mesopotamia, capital of the Babylonian empire
- אֶרֶךְ (Erech): Uruk — ancient Sumerian city-state, home of Gilgamesh, one of the oldest cities in the world (modern Warka)
- אַכַּד (Accad): Akkad — capital of Sargon's empire (location debated, probably near Kish in central Babylonia)
- כַּלְנֵה (Calneh): Uncertain; possibly Nippur, or a toponym otherwise unattested in this form. Some ancient versions read "all of them" (כֻּלָּנָה) rather than a city name.

בְּאֶרֶץ שִׁנְעָר (in the land of Shinar): The Hebrew name for Babylonia/southern Mesopotamia. Shinar appears at Genesis 11:2 (the Babel episode), 14:1, Daniel 1:2, and Zechariah 5:11 — always for the Babylonian sphere. The Sumerian or Akkadian etymology is disputed; Sumer (Šumer) has been proposed.

**10:11-12 — Nineveh and the Northern Cities: Who Built Them?**

The syntax of v. 11 is famously ambiguous:

מִן-הָאָרֶץ הַהִוא יָצָא אַשּׁוּר — "From that land Asshur went out..."

Two readings:
1. **Asshur (the person)** went out from Shinar (i.e., from Nimrod's territory) and built Nineveh and the northern cities. On this reading, Asshur (son of Shem, v. 22) is the builder of Assyria's cities — consistent with Asshur being the eponymous ancestor of Assyria.
2. **He (Nimrod) went out into Assyria** [yielding: "from that land he went out to Asshur"] — reading אַשּׁוּר not as the subject but as the destination. On this reading, Nimrod expanded his empire northward and built Nineveh himself. This reading is supported by the LXX: ἐκ τῆς γῆς ἐκείνης ἐξῆλθεν Ασσουρ — "From that land Asshur went out" — but many commentators treat the LXX as ambiguous on the same point.

The EMET translation renders "Asshur went out" as the most natural Hebrew reading (the proper name Asshur as subject of the verb). However, many traditions — and the logic of the Nimrod context — support reading Nimrod as the builder of both the southern and northern city-complexes. The ambiguity is not resolvable from the Hebrew alone.

**The cities of Assyria:**
- נִינְוֵה (Nineveh): The great Assyrian capital on the Tigris, famous as the city to which Jonah was sent; one of the largest cities of the ancient world (modern Mosul/Kuyunjik, Iraq)
- רְחֹבֹת עִיר (Rehoboth-ir): "Broad places of the city" — possibly a district of Nineveh or a satellite city; not otherwise identified
- כָּלַח (Calah): Nimrud — Assyrian Kalhu, major capital of the Neo-Assyrian empire (Ashurnasirpal II's capital); site of the famous ivory carvings and colossal winged bulls (modern Nimrud, Iraq)
- רֶסֶן (Resen): "Between Nineveh and Calah" — unidentified; possibly Resh-eni (a spring-city) or a district in the Nineveh-Calah corridor

**"That is the great city" (הִוא הָעִיר הַגְּדֹלָה, v. 12):** The antecedent of "that" is ambiguous — it may refer to Nineveh alone, to Calah, or to the entire metropolitan complex (Nineveh-Calah-Resen as a single urban agglomeration). Jonah 3:3 calls Nineveh "an exceedingly great city" — the same designation — suggesting Nineveh is the intended referent here, even if Resen is the grammatically nearest antecedent.

---

### On 10:13-14 — The Sons of Mizraim: Egypt's Peoples

The descendants of Mizraim (Egypt) are largely plural gentilics (peoples, not individuals): Ludim, Anamim, etc. These appear to be ethnic/tribal names for peoples associated with or derived from Egypt. Most cannot be identified with certainty:

- לוּדִים (Ludim): Possibly the Libyans or a Lydian connection; distinct from Lud son of Shem (v. 22)
- עֲנָמִים (Anamim): Unknown; possibly Egyptian Delta people
- לְהָבִים (Lehabim): Possibly the Libyans (Libyan: Rebu/Labu in Egyptian records)
- נַפְתֻּחִים (Naphtuhim): Possibly from Egyptian "people of Ptah" (Memphis area) or northern Egypt
- פַּתְרֻסִים (Pathrusim): Upper Egypt (Patros/Pathros — from Egyptian: "land of the south")
- כַּסְלֻחִים (Casluhim): Unknown; the parenthetical "from whom the Philistines went out" (אֲשֶׁר יָצְאוּ מִשָּׁם פְּלִשְׁתִּים) has long puzzled interpreters, since the Philistines are more commonly associated with the Caphtorim (cf. Deut. 2:23; Amos 9:7 — "Did I not bring Israel from Egypt, and the Philistines from Caphtor?"). Either the parenthetical has been displaced from its position after Caphtorim, or the Philistines had a complex dual origin involving both Caphtor (Crete) and a prior Egyptian connection via Casluh.
- כַּפְתֹּרִים (Caphtorim): Caphtor = Crete (or more broadly the Aegean; Akkadian: Kaptaru; Egyptian: Kftyw). The Sea Peoples' connection to the Philistines runs through Caphtor.

---

### On 10:15-19 — The Sons of Canaan: The Land of Promise and Its Peoples

The Canaanite section (vv. 15-19) is the most politically charged in the entire Table of Nations for the Israelite reader. These are the peoples of the land YHWH promised to Abraham's descendants — peoples who will be driven out or destroyed in the conquest narrative. Their genealogical derivation from Canaan (son of Ham) places them under the curse pronounced in 9:25.

**10:15 — צִידֹן בְּכֹרוֹ: "Sidon his firstborn"**

Sidon (modern Saida in Lebanon) is the firstborn of Canaan — the oldest and greatest Phoenician city-state. The priority of Sidon over Tyre in this list reflects the earlier period of Phoenician history when Sidon was the dominant port (Tyre later eclipsed Sidon in the Iron Age).

**חֵת (Heth):** The eponymous ancestor of the Hittites. The biblical Hittites (בְּנֵי חֵת) appear in patriarchal narratives (Abraham buys the cave of Machpelah from "the sons of Heth," Gen. 23). The relationship between the biblical Hittites and the great Hittite empire of Anatolia (Hatti, 1800-1200 BCE) is debated; they may be a different people sharing a name, or a remnant Hittite population in Canaan after the empire's collapse.

**The ten Canaanite peoples (vv. 15-18):** Jebusite (Jerusalem area), Amorite (highland Canaan and Transjordan), Girgashite (location uncertain), Hivite (Shechem area; possibly = Horites/Hurrians), Arkite (Tell Arqa, north Lebanon), Sinite (possibly Siyannu near Ugarit), Arvadite (Arwad/Ruad island, north Phoenicia), Zemarite (Sumur on the north Lebanese coast), Hamathite (Hamath on the Orontes, modern Hama, Syria).

The full list of Canaanite peoples expelled from the land is typically given as seven in Deuteronomy 7:1. The list here (ten, counting Sidon and Heth) is more expansive and represents a broader geographic conception of "Canaan."

**10:18b-19 — The Border of Canaan:**

The borders described in v. 19 define the extent of the Canaanite ethnographic world:
- **Southwest boundary:** Sidon → Gerar (south of Gaza, near the Philistine coast) → Gaza (the Mediterranean coast)
- **Southeast boundary:** toward Sodom and Gomorrah and Admah and Zeboiim — the Cities of the Plain at the southern end of the Dead Sea
- **Eastern terminus:** לָשַׁע (Lasha) — unknown; possibly a location near the Dead Sea or east of it

This description is roughly consistent with the "from Dan to Beersheba" borders of later Israelite geography, but oriented from the Canaanite perspective — and it notably extends the southern Canaanite world to include the Cities of the Plain (Sodom etc.), which are destroyed in Genesis 19.

---

### On 10:21 — Shem and the Children of Eber: The Hebrew Connection

וּלְשֵׁם יֻלַּד גַּם-הוּא אֲבִי כָּל-בְּנֵי-עֵבֶר אֲחִי יֶפֶת הַגָּדוֹל — "And to Shem also — he was the father of all the children of Eber, the elder brother of Japheth — even to him children were born."

This verse is syntactically awkward in Hebrew — the EMET translation preserves the awkwardness. The normal order of a genealogical introduction would be: "The sons of Shem are..." But instead the narrator inserts a double identification of Shem before listing his sons: (1) he is "the father of all the children of Eber," and (2) he is "the elder brother of Japheth." Both identifications are emphatic; both are loaded.

**"The father of all the children of Eber" (אֲבִי כָּל-בְּנֵי-עֵבֶר):**

עֵבֶר (Eber) is the eponymous ancestor of the עִבְרִים (Ivrim, "Hebrews"). The name עִבְרִי (Hebrew) appears to derive from עֵבֶר — "one from across, one who has crossed over" (from the root עָבַר, avar, "to cross, pass over"). The designation of Shem as "the father of all the children of Eber" — placed before even the listing of Shem's sons — signals that Eber is the most significant figure in Shem's entire line. The Shem section exists to lead to Eber, and Eber's line will lead to Peleg → Reu → Serug → Nahor → Terah → Abram (Gen. 11:10-26). Shem is introduced as Eber's ancestor because Eber is the ancestor of the Hebrews — and the Table of Nations is ultimately the genealogical map that shows where Abraham came from.

**"The elder brother of Japheth" (אֲחִי יֶפֶת הַגָּדוֹל):**

The adjective הַגָּדוֹל ("the great/elder") is syntactically ambiguous: does it modify Japheth (Shem is the elder brother of Japheth = Shem is older than Japheth) or Shem (Shem the elder brother)? The Hebrew word order (אֲחִי יֶפֶת הַגָּדוֹל) most naturally places הַגָּדוֹל in apposition to Japheth: "the brother of Japheth the Great" — meaning Japheth is the elder/great one. But this would make Japheth older than Shem, which contradicts the usual birth-order (Shem is mentioned first in 5:32; 6:10; 9:18; 10:1). The traditional understanding is that הַגָּדוֹל modifies Shem: "Shem, the elder brother, the brother of Japheth" — i.e., Shem is the elder and Japheth is younger. The LXX reads: ἀδελφοῦ Ιαφεθ τοῦ μεγάλου — "brother of Japheth the great" — which would make Japheth the elder. This is the major text-critical crux of this verse.

**EMET decision on 10:21 syntax:** The EMET translation renders "the elder brother of Japheth" as an unresolved ambiguity — whether "elder" modifies Shem or Japheth cannot be determined from the Hebrew alone, and the ancient versions disagree. The LXX supports Japheth as the elder; the traditional rabbinic reading supports Shem as the elder. Most English translations (KJV, ESV, NASB) follow the Shem-as-elder reading.

---

### On 10:22 — The Sons of Shem: A Key Variant (Asshur)

The presence of **אַשּׁוּר (Asshur)** in the Shem-list (v. 22) alongside its appearance as the destination/agent in the Nimrod passage (v. 11) reflects the dual status of Asshur in biblical tradition: Asshur is simultaneously a son of Shem (an ethnic/national category) and a figure associated with the Nimrod narrative (a geographic-political entity). The genealogical placement under Shem marks the Assyrians as a Semitic people — which they were linguistically (Akkadian is a Semitic language). The Nimrod connection in vv. 10-12 reflects Babylon's cultural domination of Mesopotamia.

**The sons of Aram (v. 23):** Uz, Hul, Gether, Mash. These represent Aramean sub-groups in the Syrian-Mesopotamian sphere. עוּץ (Uz) is the homeland of Job (Job 1:1); חוּל (Hul) and גֶּתֶר (Gether) are otherwise unidentified; מַשׁ (Mash) — 1 Chr. 1:17 reads מֶשֶׁךְ (Meshech) here, a significant variant.

---

### On 10:24 — Arphaxad Fathered Shelah: The LXX Cainan Variant

**This is the most significant textual variant in Genesis 10.**

**MT (Leningrad Codex) 10:24:**
וְאַרְפַּכְשַׁד יָלַד אֶת-שָׁלַח — "And Arphaxad fathered Shelah"

**LXX (Codex Vaticanus) 10:24:**
Αρφαξαδ δὲ ἐγέννησεν τὸν Καϊναν, Καϊναν δὲ ἐγέννησεν τὸν Σαλα — "And Arphaxad fathered Cainan, and Cainan fathered Shelah"

The LXX inserts an entire generation — **Cainan** (Καϊναν) — between Arphaxad and Shelah. This Cainan is distinct from the antediluvian Cainan (Kenan) of Genesis 5:9-14, though the names are identical. This LXX addition:

1. Is confirmed in Luke 3:36, where the Lucan genealogy of Jesus includes "Cainan son of Arphaxad" — Luke's genealogy follows the LXX, not the MT, at this point.
2. Is absent from the MT, the Samaritan Pentateuch, Jubilees (which gives the same genealogy without Cainan here), and most ancient Semitic sources.
3. The Cainan insertion is also present in Genesis 11:12-13 in the LXX, where it gives Cainan a lifespan (135/430 years) — a fully developed genealogical entry.

The origin of the LXX Cainan is disputed:
- **Secondary addition to LXX:** The LXX may have harmonized with an independent tradition, or a scribe inserted Cainan to fill a perceived chronological gap in post-flood genealogy.
- **Original reading lost from MT:** Some scholars argue the LXX preserves an older tradition that was accidentally dropped (by homoeoteleuton — the scribe's eye skipping from Arphaxad to Shelah because both end similarly) from the proto-MT tradition.
- **Jubilees evidence:** The Book of Jubilees (2nd century BCE) does not include this Cainan in its Genesis 10 genealogy, which may indicate the MT tradition predates the LXX addition. However, a Qumran fragment (4Q Jubilees) presents difficulties.

The chronological consequence of the Cainan addition is significant: the LXX's ages for the post-flood patriarchs are substantially higher than the MT's, creating a longer chronological span between the flood and Abraham. The Cainan generation adds roughly 130-230 years (depending on which LXX manuscript is followed) to this span.

**EMET position:** The EMET translation follows the MT (Leningrad Codex) and omits Cainan, while noting this as a major variant. The LXX reading (with Cainan) is the basis for Luke 3:36 and the longer post-flood chronology. Both readings have ancient attestation. The MT and Samaritan Pentateuch agree in omitting Cainan.

---

### On 10:25 — פֶּלֶג / נִפְלְגָה: The Peleg-Division Wordplay

כִּי בְיָמָיו נִפְלְגָה הָאָרֶץ — "For in his days the earth was divided."

The name פֶּלֶג (Peleg) is explicitly connected to the root פָּלַג (palag, "to divide, split"). This is one of the clearest onomastic etymologies in Genesis — the text provides its own interpretation. The narrative question is: what division is referred to?

1. **The Tower of Babel dispersion (Genesis 11):** The most common interpretation, and chronologically plausible — Peleg lives in the generation after the flood, and the Babel narrative follows immediately. The division of the earth in Peleg's days = the scattering of the nations at Babel.
2. **A geographical division:** Some ancient interpreters (Josephus, Antiquities 1.6.4) understood this as a geological or geographical division — the continental separation, or the division of lands by rivers. Josephus: "he was called Phaleg, which signifies division, because he was born at the dispersion of the nations to their several countries."
3. **The division of the nations as described in Genesis 10 itself:** The Table of Nations is itself the story of the division of the earth among Noah's sons. Peleg's days may mark the completion of this division.

The wordplay is performative: Peleg (Division) was born in the age of division, and his name memorializes it. Compare the Noah/rest wordplay (5:29), the Babel/balal wordplay (11:9), and the Perez/breach wordplay (38:29). These onomastic etymologies are a consistent stylistic device in Genesis.

---

### On 10:26-29 — The Sons of Joktan: South Arabian Peoples

Joktan (יָקְטָן) and his thirteen sons represent the most extensive tribal list in the Shem section. These are identified with south Arabian and Yemeni peoples:

- אַלְמוֹדָד (Almodad): Possibly a south Arabian tribe; location unknown
- שָׁלֶף (Sheleph): Possibly the Salapeni in south Arabia
- חֲצַרְמָוֶת (Hazarmaveth): Hadhramaut — the eastern region of Yemen, famous for frankincense; one of the clearest geographic identifications in the list
- יָרַח (Jerah): Possibly a lunar-worship city in south Arabia (ירח = moon)
- הֲדוֹרָם (Hadoram): Identified with Hauram in south Arabia by some; others relate to Hauran
- אוּזָל (Uzal): The ancient name of Sana'a (capital of Yemen) in Arabic tradition (Awzal/Azal)
- דִּקְלָה (Diklah): Possibly in the palm-grove region of Arabia (דֶּקֶל = palm tree in later Hebrew)
- עוֹבָל (Obal): Possibly in south Arabia; 1 Chr. 1:22 reads אֵיבָל (Ebal) — a significant orthographic variant
- אֲבִימָאֵל (Abimael): Unknown; possibly a south Arabian tribal name
- שְׁבָא (Sheba): The great Sabean kingdom of south Arabia (cf. 1 Kgs. 10: the Queen of Sheba); here a Semitic/Joktanite Sheba distinct from the Hamitic/Cushite Sheba of v. 7
- אוֹפִיר (Ophir): Famous gold-source for Solomon (1 Kgs. 9:28; 10:11); location debated between south Arabia, east Africa (Somalia/Zimbabwe), and India
- חֲוִילָה (Havilah): Second occurrence (cf. v. 7 — Cushite Havilah); here a Shemite/Joktanite Havilah; both may refer to overlapping gold-rich regions of Arabia
- יוֹבָב (Jobab): Possibly in south Arabia; some ancient traditions identified this as Job (the patriarch)

The thirteen sons of Joktan represent the most complete surviving biblical account of early south Arabian peoples. The correspondence with known south Arabian toponyms (Hadhramaut, Sana'a/Uzal, Sheba/Sabeans) is striking and gives this list unusual historical value.

---

### On 10:30 — "From Mesha toward Sephar, the Mountain of the East"

מִמֵּשָׁא בֹּאֲכָה סְפָרָה הַר הַקֶּדֶם — "from Mesha, as you go toward Sephar, the mountain of the east."

Both toponyms are uncertain:
- **מֵשָׁא (Mesha):** Possibly Mecca (?) or a location in northwest Arabia; occasionally identified with Massa (Gen. 25:14, a son of Ishmael)
- **סְפָרָה (Sephar):** Possibly Dhofar (the frankincense coast of Oman); the LXX reads Σωφηρα — close to Ophir

"The mountain of the east" (הַר הַקֶּדֶם) is a general direction indicator rather than a specific toponym — "the eastern highland." Together these coordinates point to the Arabian peninsula — consistent with all of Joktan's sons being Arabian peoples.

---

### On 10:32 — Conclusion: The Table of Nations as Theological Document

אֵלֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹת בְּנֵי-נֹחַ לְתוֹלְדֹתָם בְּגוֹיֵהֶם וּמֵאֵלֶּה נִפְרְדוּ הַגּוֹיִם בָּאָרֶץ אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל — "These are the families of the sons of Noah according to their generations in their nations; and from these the nations were spread abroad in the earth after the flood."

The closing formula explicitly connects the Table of Nations to the toledot (generations) structure of Genesis. The seventy nations catalogued in Genesis 10 (the traditional count: 14 Japhethite, 30 Hamite, 26 Shemite = 70, with variant counts depending on how plurals are handled) became a foundational number in Israelite theology — seventy as the number of the nations, corresponding to the seventy elders of Israel (Exod. 24:1), the seventy palm trees of Elim (Exod. 15:27), and ultimately the seventy disciples of Luke 10:1 (in manuscripts that read 70 rather than 72). Deuteronomy 32:8 in the LXX reads: "When the Most High divided the nations, when he separated the sons of Adam, he fixed the boundaries of the peoples according to the number of the sons of God" (κατὰ ἀριθμὸν ἀγγέλων θεοῦ) — the MT reads "sons of Israel" (בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל) — a major LXX variant that the Dead Sea Scrolls (4QDeut) support with "sons of God" (בְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים). The nations of Genesis 10 thus map onto a cosmic-political theology in which each nation has its own divine overseer, while YHWH's portion is Israel.

**The theological function of the Table of Nations:**

The Table of Nations is structurally unique in ancient literature. No other ancient culture produced a systematic genealogical account of the origin of all known peoples from a single ancestor. The nearest parallels (Hesiod's Theogony; the Mesopotamian king lists) are either theological without being ethnographic or ethnographic without being universal. Genesis 10 is both: a theological claim (all humanity descends from Noah, therefore from Adam, therefore from a single creation) and an ethnographic document (naming and locating the known peoples of the ancient Near Eastern world). The theological implication is radical: all peoples are kin. The divisions of language, territory, and culture that separate the nations (the theme of Genesis 11) are mapped onto a common genealogical root. Genesis 10 is the universalist preface to the particularist election narrative that begins in Genesis 12.

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 10

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Notes |
|-------|---------------------|-----------------|-------|
| 10:4 | דֹּדָנִים (Dodanim) | Ῥόδιοι (Rhodioi) | 1 Chr 1:7 also reads Rodanim; LXX = Rhodes; MT = uncertain |
| 10:9 | גִּבֹּר-צַיִד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה | γίγας κυνηγὸς ἐναντίον κυρίου | LXX renders גִּבֹּר as γίγας (giant!) — stronger than "mighty one"; ἐναντίον (before/against) — same ambiguity as MT |
| 10:11 | יָצָא אַשּׁוּר | ἐξῆλθεν Ασσουρ | LXX reads Asshur as subject (person) going out — same ambiguity as MT |
| 10:14 | וְאֶת-כַּפְתֹּרִים (Caphtorim last) | has Philistines after Caphtorim | LXX reorders: Casluhim → Caphtorim → Philistines; MT places Philistines after Casluhim |
| 10:21 | אֲחִי יֶפֶת הַגָּדוֹל | ἀδελφοῦ Ιαφεθ τοῦ μεγάλου | LXX: "brother of Japheth the Great" = Japheth is elder; MT ambiguous |
| 10:24 | אַרְפַּכְשַׁד יָלַד אֶת-שָׁלַח | Αρφαξαδ ἐγέννησεν τὸν Καϊναν, Καϊναν ἐγέννησεν τὸν Σαλα | **Major variant:** LXX adds Cainan between Arphaxad and Shelah; MT omits; Luke 3:36 follows LXX |
| 10:23 | וָמַשׁ (Mash) | Μοσοχ (Mosoch) | 1 Chr 1:17 also reads Meshech; LXX = Meshech; MT = Mash |
| 10:28 | עוֹבָל (Obal) | Εβαλ (Ebal) | 1 Chr 1:22 reads Ebal; LXX supports 1 Chr reading; MT unique |

---

## Dead Sea Scrolls and Other Versions

**Dead Sea Scrolls:** No significant Genesis 10 fragments are extant from Qumran. The Book of Jubilees (found at Qumran) retells portions of the Table of Nations with some expansions but does not include the LXX Cainan insertion, supporting the antiquity of the MT tradition at this point.

**Samaritan Pentateuch:** The SP is largely in agreement with the MT for Genesis 10 and does not include the Cainan addition. The SP occasionally differs in the genealogical numbers in Genesis 11 (consistent with its broader chronological expansions) but the Table of Nations genealogy itself is stable.

**Targum Onkelos:** The Aramaic Targum Onkelos translates Genesis 10 with occasional geographic identifications inserted: e.g., identifying Javan with יָוָן (Ionia/Greece), Madai with the Medes, and often identifying Asshur with Assyria. The Targum Pseudo-Jonathan is more expansive, offering geographic identifications and anti-Nimrod polemic (reading Nimrod as a rebel before the LORD).
